content.problemStatement:
One day, Saeed was teaching a string compression algorithm. This algorithm finds all maximal substrings which contains only one character repeated one or more times (a substring is maximal if it we cannot add one character to its left or right without breaking this property) and replaces each such substring by the string "cK", where KKK is the length of the substring and ccc is the only character it contains. For example, "aabaaa" is compressed to "a2b1a3".

Saeed wanted to check if the students understood the algorithm, so he wrote a string SSS on the board and asked the students if the algorithm is effective on SSS, i.e. if the string created by compressing SSS is strictly shorter than SSS. Help them answer this question.
constraints: S may consist of only lowercase English letters.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains a single string S.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string "YES" if the algorithm is effective on S or "NO" if it is not.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The compressed string of "bbbbbbbbbbaa" is "b10a2", which is shorter. The compressed string of "c" is "c1", which is not shorter than "c". The compressed string of "aaaaaaaaaabcdefgh" is "a10b1c1d1e1f1g1h1", which is not shorter than "aaaaaaaaaabcdefgh".
The compressed string of "abcabcabcabc" is "a3b3c3", which is not shorter than "abcabcabcabc".
The compressed string of "a" is "a1", which is not shorter than "a".

solution.approach: The approach is to iterate over the string S and find all maximal substrings which contains only one character repeated one or more times. Then, we compare the length of the compressed string with the original string to determine if the algorithm is effective.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the length of the string S
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) as we only need a constant amount of space to store the variables
